title: Pattern Matching Results 🧩
description: Using match to elegantly handle Success or Error.

slides.title: Opening the Box 📦
slides.content:
So, a function returned a `Result`. How do we actually open this protective box to get our data (or strictly handle the error) safely?

The most robust, standard way is by using a `match` expression.

slides.title: Matching on Result 🧩
slides.content: You provide dedicated code blocks to execute for each possible variant.

quizData.question: What happens if you use a `match` statement on a Result, but accidentally forget to write an arm for the `Err` case?

options.text: It silently ignores the error at runtime
The compiler throws a hard error, stating your match is non-exhaustive

quizData.explanation: Rust forces you to explicitly acknowledge and handle every single solitary variant of an Enum when matching. If an error is possible, the compiler holds you strictly accountable to deal with it.

slides.title: Quick Unpacking: unwrap() 🔨
Friendly Crashing: expect() 💡

quizData.question: What is the key difference between `.unwrap()` and `.expect("message")` on a Result type?

options.text: `unwrap` tries to fix the error, while `expect` does not
Both extract variables or successfully panic, but `expect` attaches a custom helpful developer error message.

quizData.explanation: Using `.expect()` actively leaves breadcrumbs for other developers so that if the program does crash, they can read exactly why it failed in the terminal log.
